episodeOutline.title: The First Bell
episodeOutline.synopsis: It's move-in day at Ridgeline Academy, and player has exactly one day to figure out who they're going to be here. Between a hallway collision with the school's magnetic golden-child Jordan, a lunchroom seating decision, a group project pairing with awkward outsider Ben, and a public moment that tests everyone's silence, player's small choices quietly build toward one of three very different versions of 'fitting in.'

scenes.title: The Front Steps
scenes.description: Player stands outside the school for the first time, schedule crumpled in hand, watching streams of students who all seem to already know each other. An inner monologue establishes the stakes: this is a fresh start, and nobody here knows the old version of you.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Anxious anticipation - the blank page feeling

scenes.title: Hallway Collision
scenes.description: Jordan, clearly a well-liked upperclassman, nearly knocks player's books to the floor while walking backward mid-conversation with friends. Jordan turns, notices the new face, and grins - a small crowd's attention flickers toward player for one unscripted second.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Sudden spotlight - the first-impression jolt

scenes.title: Holding Your Ground
scenes.description: Jordan seems genuinely impressed by player's easy comeback and introduces themselves properly, mentioning they'll 'see them around.' A few onlookers register player as someone worth knowing.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Small validation, cautious pride

scenes.title: Quiet Recovery
scenes.description: Player gathers the dropped books without fuss. Jordan barely notices, already turning back to friends, but a passing girl with paint-stained fingers - Maya - gives player a small, knowing half-smile before disappearing into the crowd.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Overlooked but seen by the right person

scenes.title: Trading Jokes
scenes.description: Player volleys the joke right back at Jordan, who laughs out loud and calls player 'quick' in front of friends. It gets a laugh from the group, though one boy at the edge of the circle doesn't quite laugh along.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Adrenaline of a risk that paid off

scenes.title: The Cafeteria Map
scenes.description: Tray in hand, player pauses at the edge of the cafeteria. Jordan's table is loud and central, already waving player over. Maya sits near the windows, sketchbook open, clearly settled into her own space. A handful of open seats sit scattered between groups. There's a second, maybe two, before player has to start walking somewhere.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The unspoken social math of a first lunch, before the feet even move

scenes.title: Watching Jordan's Table
scenes.description: Jordan is mid-story, hands gesturing wide, the table already laughing before the punchline lands. It looks effortless from here - the kind of belonging that doesn't have to try.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The pull of easy magnetism

scenes.title: Watching Maya
scenes.description: Maya isn't waiting to be invited over - she's got her sketchbook propped against her tray, clearly deep in her own project, entirely unbothered by eating alone. It doesn't look lonely so much as chosen.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Noticing someone who isn't asking to be noticed

scenes.title: Watching the Open Seats
scenes.description: The open seats between groups are the lowest-risk option - no group to join, no group to leave out. Safe, maybe. Also nowhere in particular.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The comfort and cost of staying uncommitted

scenes.title: Where You Sit
scenes.description: Tray still in hand, it's time to actually pick a seat. Jordan waves again. Maya hasn't looked up, but there's an open spot across from her. The in-between seats are still there too. The whole rest of the year could hinge on this one choice.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The moment before the decision becomes real

scenes.title: Sketchbook Seat
scenes.description: Maya is startled, then quietly pleased, when player sits down. 'I'm used to it,' she says, not quite looking up from her sketchbook. 'Not because I have to be - I just like my own company most days. But you're not... annoying, so.' A beat later she turns the sketchbook around: the school mascot, drawn as something absurdly tragic, and a real laugh breaks the ice.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Unexpected warmth, offered on her terms

scenes.title: Center Table
scenes.description: Jordan's friends fold player into the conversation easily - it's fast, funny, and easy to keep up with. By the time the bell's close, three people already know player's name, and someone half-promises to save a seat tomorrow. Across the room, Maya glances over once, then goes back to her sketchbook.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The real, uncomplicated ease of being welcomed in

scenes.title: Table Hopping
scenes.description: Player says a quick hello at Jordan's table, then swings by Maya's to compliment her sketchbook before finally settling somewhere in between. It's a little exhausting, but by the end of lunch, three different tables know player's name.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Exhilarating but precarious - stretched in every direction

scenes.title: Partner Assignments
scenes.description: Ms. Alvarez pairs the class for a semester-long project. Player is paired with Ben, a nervous, stammering kid who immediately apologizes for 'probably ruining the grade.' From across the room, Jordan stage-whispers something that gets a laugh from a couple of friends - though a couple of other students throw Jordan an uneasy glance before turning back to their notes.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A small cruelty in the open, and a choice about complicity

scenes.title: Standing Beside Ben
scenes.description: Player says, loud enough for the room to hear, that they're glad to be paired with Ben. Ben's shoulders drop half an inch in relief. Jordan's eyebrows lift - not annoyed, more caught off guard, like it hadn't quite landed how he meant it to. He doesn't apologize, but he doesn't push it further either.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Quiet courage, and a flicker of someone else's accountability

scenes.title: Working It Out Privately
scenes.description: Player doesn't call out Jordan's comment, but leans over to Ben afterward and starts brainstorming project ideas one-on-one. Ben visibly relaxes once the attention is off him.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Support without spectacle

scenes.title: Going Along With It
scenes.description: Player laughs along with Jordan's joke. It feels easy in the moment - until Ben glances over, and player catches the exact second his face falls.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A cheap laugh that curdles almost immediately

scenes.title: Called On
scenes.description: Ms. Alvarez asks Ben to share the group's project idea aloud. He stumbles over his words, and Jordan's table snickers loud enough for the room to hear. Ms. Alvarez glances up from the board - 'Let's keep it respectful' - then turns back to what she was writing, already half-distracted. The snickering fades but Ben is still red, still stuck mid-sentence, the room's attention lingering a beat too long.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The moment where silence becomes a choice too

scenes.title: Out Loud
scenes.description: Player speaks up, calmly backing Ben's idea and building on it. The laughter dies out fast, and the room's attention shifts to player instead. Ben exhales, visibly grateful. A couple of Jordan's friends clock player differently now - not unkindly, just... noted. Someone who pushes back.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Trust earned, and a new line quietly drawn

scenes.title: Looking Away
scenes.description: Player stays quiet in the moment, and Ben mumbles through the rest of his sentence alone. Later, packing up, player leans over with a specific idea for the project - something that actually builds on what Ben said. Ben's shoulders loosen, surprised and grateful, even if it came after the fact.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A quieter kind of loyalty, built after the fact rather than in front of everyone

scenes.title: Changing the Subject
scenes.description: Player cracks a self-deprecating joke about their own project idea, pulling the room's attention away from Ben without calling anyone out directly. The laughter shifts targets, and Ben gets a second to recover.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A sideways kindness - not brave, but not nothing

scenes.title: Last Bell
scenes.description: The day is ending. Jordan's group is heading somewhere and half-invites player along. Maya lingers nearby, sketchbook under her arm, clearly deciding whether to say something. Ben walks past alone, glancing back once. Player has one more choice about how today is going to define tomorrow.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The crossroads moment - who do you want to become here

scenes.title: The Real Thing
scenes.description: Player catches up to Ben and Maya instead, asking Ben if the project idea from class could actually work, and asking Maya to properly show off the sketchbook. It's smaller than Jordan's group, and maybe a little more effort than just coasting - but something about it feels less like performing and more like starting.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Belonging built on honesty rather than performance

scenes.title: In the Group
scenes.description: Player joins Jordan's group, and it feels genuinely good - easy laughter, immediate ease. Walking away, player notices Ben and Maya leaving separately, in different directions, and the thought passes as quickly as it arrived.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Popularity's real warmth, with a thought that lingers just a little

scenes.title: Both Worlds
scenes.description: Player tells Jordan's group they'll catch up later, then walks partway with Ben and Maya, trading numbers and half-formed plans. Nothing is fully decided yet, but player feels like they can move between these worlds without losing themselves - for now, at least.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Cautious optimism - the sense of options still open

choicePoints.prompt: Jordan almost knocked your books flying and now the hallway is watching. How do you respond?
choicePoints.context: First social moment of the day, with an audience. There's no wrong answer, but there's no neutral one either.

options.text: Laugh it off and introduce yourself with a steady handshake.
Quietly pick up your books and keep moving.
Fire back a joke of your own.

choicePoints.context: You've got a few seconds before you actually have to decide where to sit. Where does your attention go?

options.text: Jordan's table - the easy noise of a group already comfortable together.
Maya, alone by the window, sketchbook open.
The scattered open seats in between.

choicePoints.prompt: Where do you sit for your first lunch?
choicePoints.context: Jordan's table is loud and waving you over. Maya is alone by the window. There are also scattered open seats between groups.

options.text: Sit with Maya, the girl reading alone.
Say a quick hello at both, and land somewhere in between.

choicePoints.prompt: Jordan just made a joke at Ben's expense in front of the whole class. What do you do?
choicePoints.context: You've just been paired with Ben for a semester-long project. Everyone heard the comment, including Ben.

options.text: Say, loud enough for the room, that you're glad to be paired with him.
Don't call it out, but immediately start working with Ben one-on-one.

choicePoints.prompt: Ben is stumbling through his answer and Jordan's table is snickering. What do you do?
choicePoints.context: This is the moment that could shape how the rest of the year feels for Ben - and how player is read by the room.

options.text: Speak up and back Ben's idea in front of everyone.
Stay quiet now, then help Ben privately afterward.
Crack a joke about your own idea to pull attention away from Ben.

choicePoints.prompt: The day is ending. Where do you put your energy?
choicePoints.context: Jordan's group has half-invited you along. Maya is lingering nearby. Ben just walked past alone. Tomorrow starts with whatever you decide right now.

options.text: Catch up with Ben and Maya instead.
Tell Jordan's group you'll catch up later, then walk partway with Ben and Maya.

branches.name: The Authentic Path
branches.description: Player consistently chooses connection over status - sitting with Maya, defending Ben, speaking up when it costs something socially.
branches.outcome: Player ends the day with two genuine, lower-key friendships and a growing sense of self-respect. If this path holds through a day of visible stands, Ben and Maya read the final approach as consistent, and trust deepens quickly. Sets up Maya and Ben as recurring allies in future episodes.

branches.name: The Popularity Path
branches.description: Player consistently prioritizes fitting in with Jordan's group, staying comfortable and low-risk even when it means going along with the crowd around Ben.
branches.outcome: Player ends the day socially secure and instantly 'in' - it's not villainous, just a different bet about where belonging comes from. If player later reaches toward Ben and Maya unexpectedly, both read it with a flicker of surprise or wariness, unsure what changed. Any cost surfaces later, as an unresolved thread in future episodes.

branches.name: The Adaptive Path
branches.description: Player moves fluidly between social worlds, offering quieter, indirect support rather than big public stands, and refusing to fully commit to one group.
branches.outcome: Player ends the day with a foothold in multiple social circles and a flexible, still-forming sense of identity. This path is intentionally the hardest to sustain across all five choices, which is exactly what makes it interesting to replay.

emotionalArc: Anxious anticipation on the front steps
Sudden social spotlight in the hallway
The quiet math of choosing a lunch table
The charged moment of a public choice point
Reflection and self-definition at day's end

characterArcs.arc: Moves from anxious blank slate to someone with a working answer to 'who am I here' - the specific answer depends entirely on player choices.

Introduced as an easy, magnetic golden-child; subtly revealed across branches as either a genuine ally, a careless bystander who occasionally shows a flicker of self-awareness, or a low-key gatekeeper, depending on how player responds to them.

Starts self-possessed and comfortable in her own company - not isolated by default, but selective about who she lets in. Opens up meaningfully only if player extends effort toward her, planting a seed for a larger friendship arc later in the season.

Begins visibly anxious about being an easy target; his trajectory across the episode - defended aloud, quietly supported after the fact, or left exposed - sets up his season-long relationship with player.

traitMapping.description: Measured through willingness to take social risk without needing group approval first - introducing yourself, speaking up publicly, standing by an unpopular choice.
Measured through attentiveness to overlooked people and willingness to act on their behalf even at social cost - choosing Maya, defending or quietly supporting Ben, refusing to laugh along.
Measured through flexible, situational responses that don't collapse into either rigid withdrawal or total conformity - banter, table-hopping, redirecting tension with humor, quiet strategic support.

relationshipDynamics.dynamic: A test of whether player can engage with charisma and social power without losing themselves in it. Trends toward genuine mutual respect, distant cordiality, or quiet complicity depending on branch.
A slow-burn potential friendship gated entirely on player noticing and choosing her when it isn't the obvious move - built on her terms, not out of pity.
The episode's emotional core - Ben's sense of safety at this new school is directly shaped by whether player defends him aloud, supports him quietly, or leaves him exposed in front of others.

replayHooks: Players who chose the popularity path will wonder what Maya's sketchbook joke or Ben's real project idea actually was - only visible on the authentic and adaptive paths.
The three cafeteria 'notice' scenes and lunchroom scenes (5a/5b/5c) reveal different pieces of Jordan and Maya's personalities not visible in other branches.
Jordan's brief flicker of accountability in scene-7a is easy to miss and only appears if player defends Ben out loud - a hidden layer under his confidence.
Ben's arc changes meaningfully across all three choice-4 options, encouraging replay just to see how differently he responds to being defended aloud vs. supported quietly vs. redirected around.

The 'adaptive' branch is the hardest to fully achieve since it requires consistently choosing the indirect option across all five choices - players often want to replay to see if they can 'thread the needle.'
Maya's line about choosing solitude on her own terms changes how her later warmth reads - worth noticing on a second playthrough.
educationalGoals: Model confidence as steady self-assertion rather than dominance or performance
Build empathy by making perspective-taking (noticing Ben's and Maya's emotional states) a rewarded, natural part of the story rather than an explicit lesson
Illustrate adaptability as flexible authenticity - adjusting behavior to context without abandoning core values, and as a legitimate strategy, not just a compromise
Show that first impressions and social choices have consequences that ripple forward, encouraging reflective decision-making
Normalize the idea that there is no single 'correct' way to belong, while still surfacing the emotional costs of certain choices
Establish clear boundaries around unkindness (via a teacher's redirect and peers' discomfort) without turning any single choice into a scored morality test
conversationStarters: Which lunch table would you actually have sat at on your first day somewhere new?
Was there ever a moment you wished you'd spoken up for someone, like in the classroom scene?
Do you think Jordan is a good person, a bad person, or just someone figuring things out too?
Did your choices feel like 'you,' or were you playing a version of yourself you don't usually show?
Do you think Maya chose to sit alone, or did it just happen that way? Does it matter?
What do you think happens to Ben and Maya next, depending on which path you chose?

designNotes:
Key changes made in response to feedback:

1. TRANSITIONS/SCHEMA (QA blocker + major): This outline's authoritative transition model is choicePoint.options[].nextScene and scene.defaultNextScene, exactly per the TRANSITION RULES provided - there is no separate 'transition' object in this schema. Scenes 11a/11b/11c correctly have no choicePoint and a defaultNextScene of 'END', which is the non-ambiguous terminal pattern for this data model. I did not introduce a transition object since it isn't part of the required schema; if the production engine actually uses a transition/choiceId object model, that's a separate data-mapping layer downstream of this outline and should be generated from these fields, not duplicated here.

2. Added traitMapping to choice-5 (it was actually already present in the draft; kept and lightly tuned so no option carries a 'punishing' double-negative).

3. Scene 4 is now interactive: split into scene-4 (intro) + choice-2a (a 3-option 'what catches your attention' micro-choice) + three short notice scenes + scene-4-decision (the actual seating choice, formerly embedded choice-2). This gives constant agency and lets adaptability/empathy/confidence get a light early signal before the big lunch decision, per the GAME_DESIGNER note. Only one micro-scene plays per run, so play time only grows by ~1 minute.

4. Neutralized scene-5b: removed 'guilt' framing from the description and emotional beat; joining Jordan's table now reads as genuinely warm and low-cost in the moment. Also removed choice-2's ADAPTABILITY:-1 penalty on option b so no option is invisibly 'wrong.' Deferred consequences to branch-level outcome text and future-episode threads instead of narrating guilt in the moment.

5. Reframed choice-4 (Ben's public moment): 9a and 9b are now two different valid strategies (visible defense that marks you socially vs. quieter after-the-fact support that keeps you under Jordan's radar) rather than a right/wrong pair. Removed Ms. Alvarez's approving nod as an explicit reward signal; the payoff is now social/strategic (how Jordan's table reads you) rather than moral score. TraitMapping for choice-4 no longer includes negative deltas, since both paths are legitimate trait expressions.

6. Ethics fixes: (a) Jordan's behavior is now clearly marked as a boundary violation via peer discomfort in scene-6 ('a couple of other students throw Jordan an uneasy glance') and a direct teacher redirect in scene-8 ('Let's keep it respectful'), without fully resolving Ben's exposure (keeps the dilemma alive). (b) Scene-7a now includes Jordan showing a genuine flicker of recognition/accountability rather than just an eyebrow raise. (c) Maya's agency is made explicit in scene-4-notice-maya and scene-5a - she states outright that solitude is her choice, not a consequence of being unwanted, directly incorporating the suggested dialogue.

7. Convergence at the ending: given this schema doesn't support conditional/stateful scene content (no variables, just scene-to-scene chaining), full path-dependent final scenes would require a combinatorial explosion of unique scene ids for every prior-choice combination. As a proportionate fix within the current architecture, I expanded the branch outcome text (not scene text) to explicitly describe how Ben/Maya/Jordan's reactions differ depending on the path that led to scene-11a/b/c - this preserves the intended narrative nuance at the 'meta' level surfaced in conversationStarters and season-long continuity notes, without requiring a state engine. See uncertainties below for a recommended deeper fix.
uncertainties: The ending convergence (all paths funnel through the same three scene-11 options) is only partially resolved. A full fix would require either (a) a lightweight state/flag system so scene-11 text can vary based on prior choices, or (b) duplicating scene-11a/b/c into path-specific variants (e.g., scene-11a-warm vs. scene-11a-wary), which would roughly double the scene count. I chose the lighter-touch fix (branch-level outcome narration) to stay within the 10-15 minute target and avoid overengineering a single episode; flagging this as a candidate for a future engine feature (persistent trait/relationship variables) rather than a per-episode content fix.

Choice-4's reframing (9a = 'noted by Jordan's table' vs 9b = 'stays under the radar') is intended to feel strategically balanced rather than morally ranked, but there's a risk playtesters still read 9a as the 'good' option given the theme of the episode (standing up for Ben). Recommend a playtest pass specifically checking whether players perceive 9a/9b as equally valid rather than right/wrong.

Softening scene-5b (Jordan's table) to remove guilt framing may reduce the felt stakes of the popularity path within this single episode - the cost is now explicitly deferred to future episodes. This is a deliberate trade-off per the GAME_DESIGNER note, but it does mean this episode alone offers less immediate narrative payoff for the popularity branch; worth confirming that a season-level arc will actually deliver on that deferred cost.

Ms. Alvarez's redirect in scene-8 ('Let's keep it respectful') is intentionally brief and doesn't fully resolve Ben's exposure, to preserve the dilemma. There's a judgment call here about how much adult intervention is enough to establish the ethical boundary without defusing the choice point - open to adjusting the line's placement or specificity based on further ethics review.
